title: A generalized relationship between dose of greenness and mental health response
data_sources: Dose GitHub repository (extracted curves + R code)

data_scope.study_type: systematic review and meta-analysis of published dose-response literature
data_scope.quality_control: formal risk-of-bias assessment with quality-verified subsets

core_conclusion: The dose-of-greenness to mental-health relationship is best described by a generalized quadratic (inverted-U) curve rather than a linear one. Pooling 69 refitted curves, a quadratic pattern fits most published curves, and the authors identify highly beneficial and best doses for both eye-level and top-down greenness. A moderate dose of urban greenness, not the maximum, yields the greatest mental-health benefit.
why_it_matters: Overturns the linear 'more green is always better' assumption with a curvilinear optimum; gives planners concrete best-dose targets for eye-level and top-down greenness; harmonizes a fragmented, inconclusive literature; supports cost-effective and equitable green allocation; fully open data and R code.
why_nature: Generalized, transferable shape of a dose-response relationship; links the built environment to human mental well-being; strongly policy-translatable best-dose thresholds; rigorous evidence synthesis with risk-of-bias assessment and open data/code release.
added_value_to_my_research: Best-dose thresholds -> green deficit/surplus maps and dose-capped 2SFCA green-health accessibility
Explainable ML / MGWR on the 69-curve database -> context-conditioned optimal dose with attributable drivers
Mobility-weighted, GPS-trajectory exposure -> dynamic spatiotemporal green dose via multi-source fusion
Couple green dose with mental-health-service R2SFCA accessibility -> doubly-disadvantaged neighborhood index
Inverted-U as physics-informed prior -> shape-constrained GeoAI mental-health exposure model
